reasoning: The redirect target on line 420 is built entirely from admin_url() (a WordPress core function that produces a same-site admin URL) concatenated with the string literal 'true' or 'false' derived from update_option()'s boolean return value. No attacker-controlled data ever reaches $location. The function is also doubly gated: wp_verify_nonce() on line 410 must pass, then current_user_can('manage_options') on line 412 must pass — so the code is reachable only by authenticated admins. The finding's own justification concedes 'the destination is constructed from admin_url() and is not user-controlled' and frames the risk as hypothetical ('if the location construction is later modified'). Using wp_redirect() instead of wp_safe_redirect() is a best-practice / defense-in-depth issue, not an exploitable vulnerability in the current code. There is no open redirect here.
